subject: Careers

secondary_architecture_support.note: Each day can run as a 20-30 minute mini-lesson inside a larger block.

materials_control_note: Teacher-only notes stay teacher-facing. Student materials stay broad, age-appropriate, and low-risk.

lesson_overview.overview: A five-day Grade 8 mini-unit on bias that stays broader than careers while fitting alongside project work. Students study first impressions, media framing, recommendations, cognitive bias, and practical bias-awareness moves.
lesson_overview.essential_question: How can bias shape what we notice, believe, and decide, and how can we become more aware of it?

sequence.carryover: Students begin naming the difference between fact and assumption.
Students reuse framing language later in the week.
Students treat recommendations as suggestions, not decisions.
Students build language for slowing down quick thinking.
Final evidence stays in the exit ticket.

integrity_checks: Keep the unit broader than careers.
Keep examples grounded in Grade 8 life.
Keep support, core, and extension versions aligned.
Keep final evidence in the Day 5 exit ticket.

teacher_guide.big_idea: Bias includes stereotypes, framing, and cognitive shortcuts. Students need practical language and habits for noticing bias in school, media, online life, and everyday decisions.
learning_goals: I can guide students in identifying different kinds of bias.
I can keep the unit relevant to Grade 8 life.
I can use aligned variants without changing the target.
I can keep discussion safe and practical.
materials: Daily task sheet variants: Circle support, Square core, Triangle extension
Optional printed image or scenario cards
teacher_notes: Keep the tone reflective, not accusatory.
Start with broad examples before sensitive ones.
Allow light careers links without making careers the whole unit.
Redirect stereotypes toward evidence and fairness.

days.carryover_note: Students begin naming the difference between fact and assumption.

content.subtitle: Launch with first impressions and quick guesses.

content.scenario: Launch with first impressions and quick guesses.
prompts: What do people notice first?
Which ideas are facts and which are guesses?
Why can quick impressions feel true?

rows: Bias is a tendency to lean toward an idea or pattern.
An assumption is something we think is true without enough evidence.
A stereotype is an overgeneralized belief about a group.

content.task: Use the handout to sort statements as fact, assumption, stereotype, or opinion.
prompts: She is probably good at math.
The team won three games this week.
People who dress like that are unfriendly.
How can you pause before turning a guess into a fact?

task_sheet_supported.title: Day 1 Task Sheet — Circle Version
instructions: Answer each part in your own words.
Use examples, evidence, or explanation.
Connect the idea of bias to real Grade 8 life.

tasks.label: Part A — Notice
tasks.prompt: Write one quick judgment a person might make from a first impression.

tasks.label: Part B — Sort
tasks.prompt: Label statements as fact, assumption, stereotype, or opinion.

tasks.label: Part C — Pause
tasks.prompt: Finish: Before I decide, I should check ___.

embedded_supports: Use the labels fact, assumption, stereotype, opinion.
Ask: What evidence do I actually have?
success_criteria: I completed each part.
I used explanation instead of only a quick guess.
I connected the idea to real life.

days.carryover_note: Students reuse framing language later in the week.

content.subtitle: Study how images, captions, and headlines shape what people notice.

content.scenario: Study how images, captions, and headlines shape what people notice.
prompts: What do you notice first?
Who is centered?
What might be missing?

rows: Framing is the way information is presented so some parts stand out more than others.
A message can be true and still push the audience toward a certain reaction.
Bias in media often shows up through omission, emphasis, and repetition.

content.task: Use the handout to analyze what a media message is pushing.
prompts: Who is centered?
What is emphasized?
What is missing?
What question can you ask when you see a strong image or headline?

task_sheet_supported.title: Day 2 Task Sheet — Circle Version
instructions: Answer each part in your own words.
Use examples, evidence, or explanation.
Connect the idea of bias to real Grade 8 life.

tasks.label: Part A — Read the frame
tasks.prompt: Write what the audience is meant to notice first.

tasks.label: Part B — Message
tasks.prompt: Explain what message is being pushed.

tasks.label: Part C — Missing piece
tasks.prompt: Name one point of view or detail that is missing.

embedded_supports: Notice who is centered, missing, or emphasized.
Ask what the audience is being encouraged to assume.
success_criteria: I completed each part.
I used explanation instead of only a quick guess.
I connected the idea to real life.

days.carryover_note: Students treat recommendations as suggestions, not decisions.

content.subtitle: Question why recommendations can be useful without being neutral.

content.scenario: Question why recommendations can be useful without being neutral.
prompts: What gets recommended to you online?
Why might an app think you want that?
Can a recommendation be helpful and incomplete?

rows: Algorithms sort and recommend using data, rules, and patterns.
A recommendation is not the same thing as a decision.
Bias can appear when a tool overvalues some inputs and ignores context.

content.task: Use the handout to critique a recommendation, quiz result, or suggestion.
prompts: What information did the tool use?
What might it miss?
How should a person use the result wisely?
Why do people trust tool output too quickly?

task_sheet_supported.title: Day 3 Task Sheet — Circle Version
instructions: Answer each part in your own words.
Use examples, evidence, or explanation.
Connect the idea of bias to real Grade 8 life.

tasks.label: Part A — Example
tasks.prompt: Write one recommendation or result you have seen from a tool or app.

tasks.label: Part B — Inputs
tasks.prompt: List what the tool probably used.

tasks.label: Part C — Limits
tasks.prompt: Explain one thing the tool may not know.

embedded_supports: A recommendation is a suggestion, not a decision.
Ask what context is missing.
success_criteria: I completed each part.
I used explanation instead of only a quick guess.
I connected the idea to real life.

days.carryover_note: Students build language for slowing down quick thinking.

slides.title: Cognitive Biases in Everyday Life — Day 4

content.subtitle: Explore confirmation bias, halo effect, bandwagon effect, and availability bias.

content.scenario: Explore confirmation bias, halo effect, bandwagon effect, and availability bias.
prompts: Why do people like shortcuts in thinking?
When can a shortcut help?
When can it cause unfair decisions?

rows: Confirmation bias means noticing evidence that fits what you already think.
Halo effect means letting one strong impression shape your whole judgment.
Bandwagon effect means following a view because many other people hold it.
Availability bias means treating the easiest example to remember as the most common or important.

content.task: Use the handout to match scenarios to common cognitive biases.
prompts: Everyone is repeating a rumor.
One great first impression makes someone seem good at everything.
A dramatic video makes a rare event feel common.
How can naming a bias help you slow down?

task_sheet_supported.title: Day 4 Task Sheet — Circle Version
instructions: Answer each part in your own words.
Use examples, evidence, or explanation.
Connect the idea of bias to real Grade 8 life.

tasks.label: Part A — Match
tasks.prompt: Match each short scenario to a cognitive bias.

tasks.label: Part B — Explain
tasks.prompt: Explain why one match fits.

tasks.label: Part C — Slow down
tasks.prompt: Write one action that could slow that kind of biased thinking.

embedded_supports: Look for clue words like everyone, first impression, already believe, easiest example.
Explain why the label fits.
success_criteria: I completed each part.
I used explanation instead of only a quick guess.
I connected the idea to real life.

days.carryover_note: Final evidence stays in the exit ticket.

slides.title: Challenging Bias in Real Life — Day 5

content.subtitle: Build a practical bias pause routine for school, online life, and group work.

content.scenario: Build a practical bias pause routine for school, online life, and group work.
prompts: What can someone do when they notice a quick judgment forming?
How do you challenge a stereotype without starting a fight?
What habits make decisions fairer?

rows: Notice the first reaction without treating it as final truth.
Ask what evidence is missing and whose point of view is not included.
Choose a slower, fairer next step before sharing, judging, or deciding.

content.task: Use the handout to build a personal bias pause routine.
prompts: Notice
Question
Check
Choose
Where might this help you most right now?

task_sheet_supported.title: Day 5 Task Sheet — Circle Version
instructions: Answer each part in your own words.
Use examples, evidence, or explanation.
Connect the idea of bias to real Grade 8 life.

tasks.label: Part A — Notice
tasks.prompt: Write one situation where a quick judgment often happens.

tasks.label: Part B — Pause routine
tasks.prompt: Complete the four steps: Notice, Question, Check, Choose.

tasks.label: Part C — Prepare
tasks.prompt: Write one sentence about why bias awareness matters.

embedded_supports: Keep the steps short enough to use in real life.
Make your plan practical, not perfect.
success_criteria: I completed each part.
I used explanation instead of only a quick guess.
I connected the idea to real life.

exit_ticket.title: Day 5 Exit Ticket — Final Reflection
exit_ticket.prompt: Explain one kind of bias that affects everyday life and describe one practical strategy a Grade 8 student can use to become more aware of it before making a decision, judging a situation, or sharing information.
success_criteria: I named one kind of bias clearly.
I explained how it can affect real life.
I gave one practical awareness strategy.
I kept my final evidence in the exit ticket.
answer_key: Answers will vary. A strong response names a clear type of bias and gives a realistic awareness strategy.
